content.claim: Students in selective 6- and 8-year gimnazium tracks show higher raw outcomes and measurable value-added in reading and mathematical literacy compared to general-track peers, but gains appear redistributive rather than system-wide positive, confounded by composition effects (higher prior attainment and socio-economic status of admitted students).
content.context: Fresh live research through the Implementation planning lens
limitations: Value-added estimates are contested; some analyses show gains for selective-track students offset by losses for general-track students, indicating zero-sum redistribution rather than absolute gain
Composition effects vs. treatment effects are methodologically difficult to disentangle without experimental or quasi-experimental design
No published peer-reviewed citation provided in source notes; dissertation-level work has limited external validation
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
